title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
Financial Planning Basics for Beginners to Build Early Savings
Financial planning is a disciplined approach to managing money that supports both current needs and future responsibilities. It brings clarity to where money is coming from, where it is going, and how it can be protected and grown over time.
If you begin planning and saving for the future early, you will be in a better position to handle any situation life throws at you without much financial strain. Early planning also encourages disciplined spending habits, reduces reliance on debt, and lays a foundation for long-term financial security.
If you are searching for how to begin the journey of financial planning. Here are some financial planning tips for beginners to help you get started
Get a Head Start on Saving Money
Time is truly money when it comes to saving money. Saving early matters because time lets your savings grow through compounding. Aim to save at least 20% of your monthly income. An important saving tip is that the earlier you save, the less you need to rely on aggressive budgeting later on in life. It also helps build consistency and instil financial discipline.
Early Life Insurance
The earlier you buy life insurance, the lower your starting premium is likely to be. When you purchase coverage at a younger age, insurers generally offer higher coverage amounts at a lower cost because the risk profile is lower. Thus, buying life insurance early ensures you get meaningful coverage at affordable prices and truly protect your dependents' future in case of unexpected circumstances. A term plan is important for financial security, and you can choose other life insurance plans depending on your goals.
Early Health Cover
Sickness does not knock on your door before arriving. Medical emergencies can occur at any stage of life, often without warning. Having health insurance early ensures that unexpected healthcare expenses do not drain your savings or shake the foundation of your long-term financial plans.
Build an emergency fund.
Unexpected emergencies or job loss due to a layoff can instantly mess up your years of diligent financial planning. Building an emergency fund gives you financial stability when life takes an unexpected turn. By keeping money aside to cover at least three to six months of essential expenses, you protect yourself from having to borrow or getting into a debt trap and compromising on your financial goals due to such emergencies.
Retirement planning
Retirement might feel like a far-off destination right now, but delaying planning only makes it harder later. Starting early allows you to spread your savings over a longer period and enjoy the benefit of compounding returns, making retirement planning more manageable and less stressful. It also helps you develop discipline that supports long-term financial security.
Optimise tax planning
Effective tax planning starts with understanding how your finances can be structured to minimise tax legally. You can choose eligible investments that qualify for deductions under Sections 80C, the right tax regime, and other tax-planning strategies to optimise taxes while building an effective financial plan.
Key Takeaways
Financial planning works best when started early. Time allows savings to grow gradually, reduces financial pressure later, and makes long-term goals easier to achieve.
Aim to save at least 20% of your monthly income from the beginning. Early and consistent saving builds discipline and reduces the need for aggressive budgeting later in life.
Purchasing life insurance at a younger age helps you secure higher coverage at lower premiums, ensuring meaningful financial protection for your dependents.
Health emergencies are unpredictable. Early health insurance protects your savings and prevents medical expenses from disrupting your long-term financial plans.
Setting aside three to six months of essential expenses provides financial stability during job loss, medical emergencies, or unexpected disruptions.
Early retirement planning spreads the savings burden over time, while tax-efficient planning, including insurance-related deductions under Sections 80C and 80D, helps you retain more income legally.
Conclusion
If you are just beginning your career, now is the right time to start saving and building a financial plan. Early on, you have the advantage of time, which allows small, consistent savings to grow without pressure.
This is also the stage where forming disciplined habits around saving and protection pays off in the long run. Including life insurance plans can support long-term financial security by offering affordable coverage that grows with your responsibilities. So, utilise these financial tips for beginners and start planning your finances effectively.
FAQs
What are the basic financial goals beginners should set first?
Basic financial goals beginners should set first include building an emergency fund, starting regular savings, securing essential life and health insurance, and planning for long-term goals such as retirement.
How does budgeting help in financial planning?
By creating a budget, you can plan your financial goals, structure income, avoid overspending, and save accordingly.
Should beginners focus on saving or investing first?
Beginners should focus on saving first. Saving is the first step of the investment journey; it creates a safety net and prevents the need to withdraw investments during emergencies.
How can beginners create and stick to a monthly budget?
Beginners can create a monthly budget by tracking income and expenses, setting clear spending limits, and reviewing the budget regularly to stay consistent.
What common financial mistakes should beginners avoid?
Beginners should avoid common mistakes like not tracking expenses, not saving and investing money for the future, not getting insured early in life, making impulsive financial decisions, etc.
Disclaimer
The content provided is for general informational purposes only. The material is compiled from publicly available sources, internal insights, and other information deemed reliable. While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. The opinions expressed do not constitute formal recommendations, professional advice, or definitive guidance. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering.
full_pdf_text:
Financial Planning Basics for Beginners to Build Early Savings
Financial planning is a disciplined approach to managing money that supports both current needs and future responsibilities. It brings clarity to where money is coming from, where it is going, and how it can be protected and grown over time.
If you begin planning and saving for the future early, you will be in a better position to handle any situation life throws at you without much financial strain. Early planning also encourages disciplined spending habits, reduces reliance on debt, and lays a foundation for long-term financial security.
If you are searching for how to begin the journey of financial planning. Here are some financial planning tips for beginners to help you get started
1. Get a Head Start on Saving Money
Time is truly money when it comes to saving money. Saving early matters because time lets your savings grow through compounding. Aim to save at least 20% of your income towards goals.2 An important saving tip is that the earlier you save, the less you need to rely on aggressive budgeting later on in life. It also helps build consistency and instil financial discipline.
2. Early Life Insurance
The earlier you buy life insurance, the lower your starting premium is likely to be. When you purchase life insurance coverage at a younger age, life insurers generally offer higher coverage amounts at a lower premium because the risk profile is lower. Thus, buying life insurance early ensures you get comprehensive coverage at affordable prices and truly protect your dependents' financial future in case of unexpected circumstances. A term plan is important for financial security, and you can choose other life insurance plans depending on your life goals.
3. Early Health Cover
Sickness does not knock on your door before arriving. Medical emergencies can occur at any stage of life, often without warning. Having health insurance early ensures that unexpected healthcare expenses do not drain your savings or shake the foundation of your long-term financial plans.
4. Build an emergency fund.
Unexpected emergencies or job loss due to a layoff can instantly mess up your years of diligent financial planning. Building an emergency fund gives you financial stability when life takes an unexpected turn. By keeping money aside to cover at least three to six months of essential expenses, you protect yourself from having to borrow or getting into a debt trap and compromising on your financial goals due to such emergencies.1
5. Retirement planning
Retirement might feel like a far-off destination right now, but delaying planning only makes it harder later. Starting early allows you to spread your savings over a longer period and enjoy the benefit of compounding returns, making retirement planning more manageable and less stressful. It also helps you develop financial discipline that supports long-term financial security.
6. Optimise tax planning
Effective tax planning starts with understanding how your finances can be structured to minimise tax liability. You can choose eligible investments that qualify for deductions under Sections 80C, the right tax regime, and other tax-planning strategies to optimise taxes while building an effective financial plan.
Key Takeaways
1. Financial planning works best when started early. Time allows savings to grow gradually,
reduces financial pressure later, and makes long-term goals easier to achieve. 2. Aim to save at least 20% of your income from the beginning.2 Early and consistent saving
builds discipline and reduces the need for aggressive budgeting later in life. 3. Purchasing life insurance at a younger age helps you secure comprehensive coverage at
lower premiums, ensuring meaningful financial protection for your dependents. 4. Health emergencies are unpredictable. Early health insurance protects your savings and
prevents medical expenses from disrupting your long-term financial plans. 5. Setting aside three to six months of essential expenses provides financial stability during job
loss, medical emergencies, or unexpected disruptions.1 6. Early retirement planning spreads the savings burden over time, while tax-efficient planning,
including deductions under Sections 80C and 80D, helps you reduce your tax liability .
Conclusion
If you are just beginning your career, now is the right time to start saving and building a financial plan. Early on, you have the advantage of time, which allows small, consistent savings to grow without pressure.
This is also the stage where forming disciplined habits around saving and protection pays off in the long run. Including life insurance plans can support long-term financial security by offering affordable coverage that grows with your responsibilities. So, utilise these financial tips for beginners and start planning your finances effectively.
FAQs
1. What are the basic financial goals beginners should set first?
Basic financial goals beginners should set first include building an emergency fund, starting regular savings, securing essential life and health insurance, and planning for long-term goals such as retirement.
2. How does budgeting help in financial planning?
By creating a budget, you can plan your financial goals, structure income, avoid overspending, and save accordingly.
3. Should beginners focus on saving ?
Saving is the first step of the investment journey; it creates a safety net and prevents the need to withdraw investments during emergencies.
4. How can beginners create and stick to a monthly budget?
Beginners can create a monthly budget by tracking income and expenses, setting clear spending limits, and reviewing the budget regularly to stay consistent.
5. What common financial mistakes should beginners avoid?
Beginners should avoid common mistakes like not tracking expenses, not saving and investing money for the future, not getting insured early in life, making impulsive financial decisions, etc.
Disclaimer
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions.
